artifacts.content:
Hi Rosa,

Kids are finally in bed and I’m wiped, but I wanted to write while it’s quiet. Miguel’s SAT prep at East Ridge Learning Center is going okay, I think. He came home annoyed about the practice math section but then spent another hour on Khan Academy, so you know he cares. Diego actually sent $120 by Zelle from that Syracuse Community Credit Union account on Sunday to help with the next round of classes. I never know if it’ll be $100, $150, or nothing, depending on his overtime at the warehouse, but for once I’m grateful he picked up extra shifts.

Money still feels like trying to plug holes in a sinking boat. Mami slipped another $75 into my ESL Federal Credit Union account after her Park Avenue weekend client a couple weeks ago. She called on the bus home and said, “No le digas a tu papá, ¿sí?” and I promised. I used it to keep RG&E off my back and grab a few things at Wegmans until SNAP refills.

On top of that, Karen in 2A came over last Thursday. We sat at my dining table again with her New York State unemployment portal open on my old laptop and Indeed.com in another tab. She pressed $40 into my hand when we finished her recertification and two job applications. She keeps saying, “Please don’t tell anyone, I don’t want to mess up my benefits,” so I just think of it as gas and grocery money.

Anyway, that’s my little underground economy update. Saturday if you’re free, maybe we can do arroz con pollo here and let the kids watch a movie? I’ll make coffee; you bring your chisme.

Besos,
Elena

Budget / health brain dump – updated 12/2024

1. My own meds: since Dr. Angela Ruiz at Eastside Family Health Center added that second antidepressant back in October 2024, the mental health prescriptions alone are about $65 every month. That’s just the antidepressants + clonazepam, picked up first week of each month at the CVS on Monroe Ave, not counting the cancer meds.

2. Strong Memorial ER bill from March 18, 2024 chest pain visit (panic attack). On a $50/month payment plan with the Strong Memorial Hospital billing office. Already missed November’s payment and they warned about collections if I fall behind again. Need to call them before the 15th.

3. Pelvic floor PT with Kelly Harmon at URMC: since June 2024, I’ve been trying to go every other month for the incontinence and pelvic pain from chemo. Two visits each time, $45 copay per visit = $90 every other month. Helps, but hard to justify when RG&E is breathing down my neck.

4. Miguel: counseling with Dr. Lauren Pierce at Brighton High School, every two weeks. I pay $40 each session, so about $80/month. Worth it — his panic attacks about my cancer were getting scary this spring.

5. Medical marijuana: about $35/month since July 2024 for tincture/gummies at Columbia Care on University Ave. It’s the only thing that lets me eat during the bad chemo weeks.

Reminder to self: total all this with the oncology stuff so I’m not surprised when the bank balance looks awful. Also, try not to beat myself up for needing this much help to function.

Mami,

Solo tengo un ratito antes de ir a buscar a Isabella, but I wanted to say thank you again. I saw the $75 you put into my ESL Federal Credit Union account after your Park Avenue shift on Sunday. I used it right away for the RG&E bill and some groceries at Wegmans — we were down to rice, beans, and frozen peas.

Please don’t worry, I won’t tell Papá you’re using your own Social Security and those extra hours with your home health client to help me. I know he’d say you’re working too much already. But without you, I don’t know how I would have kept the lights on these last months.

The kids say hi. Miguel is buried in SAT prep again. Diego sent $100 from his Syracuse Community Credit Union account last month for the East Ridge Learning Center classes, but then nothing this month, so I’m trying to stretch what we have. Isabella is practicing her flute for the Twelve Corners concert — she squeaks less now, jajaja.

My body is tired. Between Strong Memorial for the hematology/oncology stuff and the new panic attacks, I feel like I live in waiting rooms. Dr. Ruiz changed my antidepressants in October and now I’m at the CVS on Monroe every month paying more copays. Some days I just lie on the gray couch and listen to Isabella’s music and breathe.

We’ll call you and Papá on Sunday like always so you can see the kids’ faces. Te quiero mucho, and thank you for helping me even when you’re tired.

Con cariño,
Elena

Event: Miguel counseling w/ Dr. Lauren Pierce (bring $40)
Date: Wednesday, 2024-12-11
Time: 3:30–4:15 pm
Location: Brighton High School – Counseling Office
Attendees: Elena, Miguel, Dr. Pierce

Notes:

– Leave apartment (4821 Willow Creek Dr, Apt 3B) by 3:00 pm. Bus if feeling okay; otherwise text Rosa for a ride.
– Remember $40 copay in envelope in purse (this is every two weeks, so about $80/month just for him).
– Ask Dr. Pierce about Miguel’s panic attacks getting worse whenever I have an infusion at Strong Memorial Hospital. He said last time he keeps imagining me back in the March 18 ER room when they thought it might be my heart.
– Mention that he’s also stressed about money: he overheard me on the phone with Strong’s billing office about the $50/month payment plan from that same March 18, 2024 emergency visit for chest pain/panic attack, and about them warning me after I missed November’s payment.
– After session, quick stop at CVS on Monroe Ave if energy allows to pick up my mental health meds (since October the antidepressant + clonazepam are about $65/month) and maybe a cheap snack for Miguel before he starts homework.
– Dinner idea: leftover arroz con pollo; ask Miguel to chop veggies if I’m too wiped.

Thread with: “Marisol – Rochester”
Date: 2024-11-09

Elena: Hey, you still coming by tomorrow afternoon? Isabella wants to show you her new flute piece and Miguel needs someone other than me to talk soccer with.

Marisol: Yes! After church, around 2? Need me to bring anything?

Elena: If you pass a Wegmans, maybe a loaf of bread? Pastor Luis at Nuestra Señora de la Paz gave us one of those $50 Wegmans cards from the mercy fund again about 3 weeks ago when he heard SNAP ran out early, so basics are covered, but I forgot bread.

Marisol: Of course. How are you holding up?

Elena: Honestly? Tired. The chemo nausea was bad this week. I finally renewed my medical marijuana card in July and now I budget about $35/month for a tincture from Columbia Care on University Ave. It’s the only thing that lets me eat some days, but it’s another bill.

Elena: And then there’s Miguel. He started seeing the school psychologist, Dr. Pierce, every two weeks this spring because he was having panic attacks about my cancer. I pay $40 every visit. Between that, my own therapist, and all the Strong Memorial stuff, I feel like our life is just appointments and copays.

Marisol: That’s a lot, amiga. I’m glad Miguel has someone to talk to. And you too.

Elena: Yeah. Some days are okay, we just sit on the couch, watch cooking videos, and I help Karen in 2A with her NYS unemployment recertification and Indeed job apps at my dining table so she slips me $40–$60. It’s weird to rely on little things like that, but it helps.

Marisol: I’ll bring bread and maybe a dessert. See you tomorrow. 💛

Personal to-do / reminder list – late November 2024

Home & kids
– Check with Miguel about next East Ridge Learning Center SAT practice schedule. If Diego sends anything extra from his Syracuse Community Credit Union account this month ($100–$150 when he gets overtime at the warehouse), set it aside just for those classes.
– Isabella: help her practice flute for Twelve Corners concert. Ask Rosa if she can drive us that night.
– Text Karen (Apt 2A) about when she wants to come by to do her New York State unemployment recertification again. She usually gives me $40–$60 cash once or twice a month when we sit at the dining table and go through the online portal and Indeed.com listings.

Money
– Log into ESL Federal Credit Union and double-check balance. Mami usually sneaks in $75 every other month after her Park Avenue home health weekend, but I shouldn’t count on it.
– Call Strong Memorial Hospital billing about the March 18, 2024 ER bill. I missed the November $50 payment and don’t want them to send it to collections.

Health
– Schedule pelvic floor PT with Kelly Harmon at URMC for December (every other month, $45 copay per visit, two visits = $90).
– CVS Monroe: refill antidepressants + clonazepam (about $65/month since October) and check if any oncology meds are due.
– Plan Columbia Care run for MMJ tincture (budget $35).

Emotional
– Write in journal one night this week instead of doom-scrolling.
– Ask Miguel after his next session with Dr. Pierce how he feels about continuing every two weeks.

Hi Pastor Luis,

I hope you’re well and that things at Nuestra Señora de la Paz are a little calmer now that we’re past the big fall festival. The kids and I haven’t made it to Mass the last two Sundays because my treatment week wiped me out, but we’ve been lighting a candle at home and watching the livestream.

I wanted to say thank you again for the $50 Wegmans grocery gift card you gave us from the mercy fund back in February, and the ones you’ve quietly slipped me every 6–8 weeks since when you hear that our SNAP ran out early. I know you make a note with my name in the parish assistance log, and I’m grateful you remember us when there are so many families struggling.

That last card helped me stretch things when Miguel’s counseling bill came due. He’s been seeing Dr. Pierce, the school psychologist at Brighton High, every two weeks since spring because of panic attacks about my cancer, and I pay $40 each visit. On top of that, my own panic attacks sent me to the Strong Memorial ER on March 18 when I had chest pain and thought it was my heart. Now I’m on a $50/month payment plan for that visit and already fell behind once.

Some days I feel embarrassed needing so much help — from the church, from my mom slipping $75 into my ESL account, from neighbors bringing over leftovers — but then I remember what you said: “We are a body; when one part is weak, the others carry it.” I repeat that to myself on the bus to Strong.

Thank you for carrying us more than you know.

Con gratitud,
Elena Martinez

From: Elena R. Martinez <elena.r.martinez81@example.com>
To: Rosa Delgado <rosadelgado73@example.com>
Date: 2025-02-03 8:19 PM
Subject: Question about that rent credit and this assistance form

Rosa,

I’m filling out this new financial assistance packet they gave me at Strong for the hematology clinic bills, and there’s a question that’s confusing me: “Additional income – please specify.” I listed the babysitting money from the upstairs neighbors (I put around $150 a month), but I don’t know what to do about what happened with Mark Henderson back in October 2023.

Do you remember when I was two months behind on rent here at 4821 Willow Creek and I was panicking about getting a 3-day notice? Mark said instead of a formal eviction, he’d let me work off $200 by cleaning those vacant units and doing some filing in his small landlord’s office on Monroe Avenue over three Saturdays. He called it a “$200 rent credit” and put it straight onto my rent ledger instead of giving me a paycheck. He also specifically asked me not to mention it to anyone because he didn’t want to deal with payroll taxes for “such a small amount.”

Does that count as income if it never hit my bank account and was just a credit against what I owed? I don’t want to get in trouble for leaving it off, but I also don’t want to mess up my chances at getting help with these oncology copays and the Strong payment plan.

Can you ask Carlos what he thinks, or if we should mention it when we meet with that social worker next week?

Love you,
Elena

metadata.subject: Question about that rent credit and this assistance form

artifacts.content:
Sticky note in tax folder – written by Elena on 2025-02-10

To do before tax appointment (H&R Block on Monroe Ave):

1. Ask about whether I need to report the $200 rent credit from Mark Henderson (landlord at 4821 Willow Creek) from Oct / October 2023.
   - Situation: I was two months behind on rent and Mark offered to let me work it off instead of starting eviction.
   - I spent three Saturdays cleaning vacant units in our building and in the other property he owns, plus doing some basic filing in his landlord’s office on Monroe Avenue.
   - He treated it as off-the-books work: no paycheck, no pay stub, just a $200 credit on my rent ledger.
   - He told me not to mention it because he didn’t want to deal with payroll taxes for such a small amount.

2. Clarify if that kind of off-the-books work to clean vacant units and do filing is considered “income” even if it was only used to cover back rent and never went into my bank account.

3. Double-check whether leaving it off could cause problems if the IRS compares anything to my SNAP case or the hospital charity care application where they ask about additional income.

4. If it *does* count as income, ask how to document it since I have no 1099, no W-2, just my rent ledger showing the $200 credit in October 2023.

metadata.title: Tax Questions – Rent Credit from Mark
